Now, let's turn to the second tumor, turnover. For business leaders, the expenditures associated with employee turnover can be a financial nightmare. Depending on the type of employee lost in the process, charges to the bottom line range from large to eye-wateringly large. Estimates of direct expenses of turnover, severance pay, recruitment, hiring, and the training of replacements can extend from 50% of the employee's entire annual compensation package for lower level positions to over 200% of the total package for executive level positions. Now, how is this economic toll related to unethical work practices? We thought it would be through the ethical stress experienced by employees whose personal values conflicted with regular wrongdoing sanctioned by the organization. One reason an honest person would want to leave a work unit would be if staying meant having to be party to consistent dishonesty. To test this possibility, we constructed two experiments plus another national survey. The results were clear. Not only were employees of unethical organizations more likely to feel stressed and to want to quit, it was the stress that was pushing them to leave. The impact of an unethical work culture on turnover is an especially consequential fiscal malignancy, because it leads to another set of financial penalties in the form of our third tumor, employee fraud and malfeasance.
